# Migration Plan: Java & Spring Upgrades

Generated by: Planning Coordinator
Date: 2026-06-17

Summary
- Goal: Upgrade the project runtime and frameworks to supported, actively maintained versions to restore OSS support, remove deprecated API usage, and reduce security and maintenance risk.
- Scope: Java runtime upgrade, Spring Framework upgrade, Spring Boot upgrade, and replacement/compatibility fixes for deprecated Common Annotations (`java.annotation`) removal.

Rationale
- The assessment identified that the project runs on a Java version and framework versions that have reached end-of-support. Upgrading restores security patches, compatibility with modern libraries, and access to improvements in performance and observability.

Assumptions
- Source builds with Maven. Tests exist and are runnable (`mvn test`).
- CI pipeline can be updated to include the new JDK once upgrades are validated.
- Teams can allocate time to update dependent libraries if required.

Approach (Planning Coordinator conventions)
1. Prepare a reproducible upgrade branch and CI sandbox to perform each upgrade. Each task maps to an isolated branch and a verification checklist.  
2. Perform the Java runtime upgrade first (minimize JVM compatibility surprises).  
3. Upgrade framework components (Spring Framework and Spring Boot) after the runtime upgrade, following compatibility matrices and incremental major/minor bump strategy.  
4. Replace or shim deprecated Common Annotations usage (the `java.annotation` removal) during or immediately after the Java upgrade; prefer library replacements (e.g., `jakarta.annotation` or dependency on the `javax.annotation` artifact) where appropriate.  
5. Run full test suite, static analysis, and smoke tests; fix compatibility issues; iterate until acceptance criteria are met.  

High-level Phases
- Phase A — Preparation: create upgrade branches, add CI JDK matrix entries, run baseline tests.  
- Phase B — Java Upgrade: upgrade JDK, fix compilation/runtime issues, run tests.  
- Phase C — Framework Upgrades: upgrade Spring Framework then Spring Boot (or in the order required by compatibility), fix code and dependency issues, run tests.  
- Phase D — Deprecated API Remediation: replace removed `java.annotation` usages and verify behavior.  
- Phase E — Validation & Release: run integration, E2E and performance smoke tests; update documentation and CI; merge and roll out.

Risks & Mitigations
- Risk: Upgrading Java exposes binary incompatibilities. Mitigation: run baseline compatibility tests, use multi-JDK CI, and maintain a rollback branch.  
- Risk: Third-party libs may not support target versions. Mitigation: inventory dependencies and update blockers first; consider temporary compatibility layers.

Rollback Strategy
- Keep the current main branch untouched. Merge only after green CI on upgrade branch. If severe regressions occur, revert the merge and open a follow-up investigation task.

Acceptance Criteria (per task)
- Build completes with the target JDK and updated dependencies.  
- All unit tests pass (or regressions are documented and triaged).  
- Application starts and passes smoke/integration checks in an isolated environment.  
- No remaining usages of removed APIs in production code (for deprecated API remediation).

Next Steps
- Execute tasks in `tasks.json` (one task per selected solution). Each task includes an effort estimate, dependencies and acceptance criteria. Use the upgrade branch naming convention: `upgrade/<task-id>-<short-title>`.

Artifacts
- Tasks list: .github/modernize/report-20260617044453/tasks.json
